tasks.spec:
You are a senior software architect. Design the ARCHITECTURE and LOGIC FLOW of a dedicated compliance 'judge/gatekeeper' layer that sits as a BRIDGE (interceptor pattern) on every message in and out of an AI system for a UK, FCA-regulated firm (context: a customer-facing AI assistant, e.g. car-leasing/financial services). Write ONLY the file ./report_arch.md in your current directory. Invent nothing about regulation beyond the GROUNDED FACTS below (gathered live from the web by the orchestrator, with sources); you may reason freely about SOFTWARE architecture.

GROUNDED FACTS (2026):
- The interceptor/proxy pattern is the established shape: a stateless proxy intercepts every LLM request, inspects/transforms it, forwards a clean version, and inspects/transforms the response — a single trust boundary that is the natural place to enforce policy, rate-limit, audit and instrument (Microsoft 'PII Shield' FastAPI proxy; LiteLLM guardrails hooks). Source: https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/blog/azuredevcommunityblog/introducing-pii-shield-a-privacy-proxy-for-every-llm-call/4514726 , https://docs.litellm.ai/docs/proxy/guardrails/pii_masking_v2
- NeMo Guardrails models the same idea as staged 'rails': INPUT rails (before the LLM), DIALOG rails (across turns), OUTPUT rails (after the LLM) — a clean mental model for a bidirectional gate. Source: https://developer.nvidia.com/nemo-guardrails

REQUIRED CONTENT (use these EXACT section headings):
## Architecture — the bridge/interceptor design: where the layer sits (client <-> judge <-> LLM, and LLM <-> judge <-> external tools/outbound), why an interceptor/proxy vs inline library, statelessness, single-boundary rationale. Include a text/mermaid diagram of the bridge topology.
## Logic Flow — the judge state machine for a single message: INSPECT -> (a) PASS THROUGH if compliant, (b) BLOCK & REJECT if hard-non-compliant, (c) RETURN FOR CORRECTION with an explanation if fixable. Cover BOTH directions (inbound customer->system and outbound system->customer). Include a mermaid flow diagram.
## Placement & Modes — sync vs async, inbound vs outbound differences, latency budget, and FAIL-CLOSED vs FAIL-OPEN policy (a compliance gate must fail closed; justify and note the availability trade-off).
## Failure Modes — what happens when the judge itself errors/times out, ordering of checks (cheap deterministic first), and how to avoid the gate becoming a single point of failure.

Strict on substance (the interceptor/bridge rationale, the 3-way judge decision, fail-closed) — a reviewer will grade whether the design is real and coherent.
tasks.check: f=report_arch.md; grep -qiE '^##[[:space:]]*Architecture' $f && grep -qiE 'interceptor|bridge|proxy' $f && grep -qiE '^##[[:space:]]*Logic Flow' $f && grep -qiE 'inspect' $f && grep -qiE 'pass' $f && grep -qiE 'block' $f && grep -qiE 'correct' $f && grep -qiE 'fail.?closed|fail.?open' $f && grep -qiE 'mermaid|-->|->' $f && echo 'arch report well-formed' || { echo 'FAIL: report_arch.md must have ## Architecture (interceptor/bridge) + ## Logic Flow (inspect/pass/block/correct) + fail-closed + a diagram'; cat $f 2>/dev/null; exit 1; }
tasks.verified: report_arch.md: bridge/interceptor architecture + 3-way judge logic flow (both directions) + fail-closed modes + diagrams, grounded in the proxy/rails facts

tasks.spec:
You are a RegTech compliance engineer. Design the COMPLIANCE RULE ENGINE and TOOLSETS for a message 'judge' layer at a UK FCA-regulated firm. Write ONLY ./report_rules.md. Use ONLY the GROUNDED FACTS below for regulation (gathered live by the orchestrator, with sources) — do not invent rules; reason freely about engineering.

GROUNDED FACTS (2026):
FCA:
- The FCA is technology-neutral and principles-based; it reaffirmed in Dec 2025 it will NOT create AI-specific rules and relies on existing frameworks: the Consumer Duty, SM&CR, operational resilience. AI-generated communications (chatbot replies, automated emails) MUST be 'clear, fair and not misleading'. Source: https://www.fca.org.uk/firms/ai-financial-services , https://www.fca.org.uk/publication/corporate/ai-update.pdf
- Consumer Duty PRIN 2A (esp. PRIN 2A.5 consumer-understanding outcome): communicate to retail customers in a way that is 'clear, fair and not misleading'; tailor to customers with characteristics of VULNERABILITY; give risks equal prominence to benefits; avoid jargon; test comprehension. (PRIN 2A last updated 26/06/2026; FCA 13 Mar 2026 consumer-understanding review.) Source: https://handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/prin2a , https://www.fca.org.uk/publications/good-and-poor-practice/consumer-understanding-good-practice-areas-improvement
- Financial promotions regime (s21 FSMA): promotions must be identifiable and approved/compliant.
UK GDPR / ICO:
- Article 22: individuals have rights around solely-automated decisions with legal/significant effects — the right to obtain HUMAN INTERVENTION, to make representations, and to contest. Human review must be MEANINGFUL: the reviewer is trained/qualified to understand the system's logic and outputs, and review happens AFTER the decision and relates to the actual outcome (not tokenistic). The Data (Use and Access) Act (DUAA) amended the ADM provisions; ICO draft ADM guidance consultation opened 31 Mar 2026, final expected summer 2026. Source: https://ico.org.uk/for-organisations/uk-gdpr-guidance-and-resources/individual-rights/automated-decision-making-and-profiling/ , https://www.insideprivacy.com/united-kingdom-2/uk-ico-consults-on-draft-automated-decision-making-guidance-and-sets-expectations-for-adm-in-recruitment/
- Core principles: lawful basis, DATA MINIMISATION, purpose limitation, transparency.
Toolsets (2026, open-source):
- Microsoft Presidio: PII/PHI detection + anonymisation (regex recognisers + spaCy NER, pluggable; redact/hash/mask/encrypt) BEFORE data hits logs or the LLM context window — 'satisfies GDPR by construction, not audit-after'. Source: https://explainx.ai/blog/microsoft-presidio-pii-detection-anonymization-guide-2026
- NeMo Guardrails (NVIDIA, Apache-2.0): programmable input/dialog/output rails; jailbreak & prompt-injection filtering, content moderation, intent classification, AlignScore fact-checking. Source: https://developer.nvidia.com/nemo-guardrails
- Llama Guard (Meta): fine-tuned LLM input/output safety classifier, 6-category taxonomy, 1B/8B open weights. Guardrails AI: Apache-2.0 validator library. Source: https://www.digitalapplied.com/blog/llm-guardrails-production-safety-layers-reference-2026

REQUIRED CONTENT (EXACT headings):
## FCA Rule Map — concrete, checkable policies the judge enforces derived from 'clear, fair and not misleading', vulnerability tailoring, risk/benefit balance, financial-promotion identification. For each: what the judge checks and the fail action.
## GDPR Rule Map — PII/data-minimisation checks, Article 22 handling (flag decisions needing a human path), lawful-basis/purpose signals. For each: check + fail action.
## Toolset Recommendation — a table mapping each concern to a concrete tool (Presidio for PII, NeMo/Llama Guard/Guardrails-AI for content & injection, an LLM-as-judge for nuanced FCA fairness), with why.
## Layered Detection — the cost-ordered pipeline: deterministic (regex/Presidio) -> fast classifier (Llama Guard) -> LLM-as-judge for nuance, so cheap checks gate expensive ones. Note precision/recall & false-positive handling.

Every regulatory claim must trace to a fact above; cite the rule names (Consumer Duty / 'clear, fair and not misleading' / Article 22 / data minimisation) and name the tools.
tasks.check: f=report_rules.md; grep -qiE '^##[[:space:]]*FCA Rule Map' $f && grep -qiE 'clear,?[[:space:]]*fair' $f && grep -qiE 'Consumer Duty' $f && grep -qiE 'vulnerab' $f && grep -qiE '^##[[:space:]]*GDPR Rule Map' $f && grep -qiE 'Article[[:space:]]*22|Art\.?[[:space:]]*22' $f && grep -qiE 'data[[:space:]]*minimi' $f && grep -qiE 'Presidio' $f && grep -qiE 'NeMo|Llama Guard|Guardrails' $f && grep -qiE '^##[[:space:]]*Layered Detection' $f && echo 'rules report well-formed' || { echo 'FAIL: report_rules.md must map FCA (clear/fair/not-misleading, Consumer Duty, vulnerability) + GDPR (Article 22, data minimisation) to checks and name tools (Presidio, NeMo/Llama Guard) with a layered pipeline'; cat $f 2>/dev/null; exit 1; }
tasks.verified: report_rules.md: FCA + GDPR rule maps to concrete judge checks, toolset table (Presidio/NeMo/Llama Guard), cost-ordered layered detection — all traceable to the grounded facts

tasks.spec:
You are a systems designer specialising in human-AI interaction and compliance UX. Design the DUAL FEEDBACK LOOPS for a compliance 'judge' layer: how a blocked/correctable message is explained DIFFERENTLY to a human customer vs to the LLM that produced it. Write ONLY ./report_feedback.md. Use ONLY the GROUNDED FACTS below for regulation; reason freely about design.

GROUNDED FACTS (2026):
- FCA Consumer Duty (PRIN 2A): customer communications must be 'clear, fair and not misleading', tailored to vulnerability, jargon-free. So a CUSTOMER-FACING explanation must be polite, plain-English, non-technical, and must NOT leak internal rule IDs, model internals, or anything that itself breaches fairness. Source: https://handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/prin2a
- UK GDPR Article 22: where a solely-automated decision has a legal/significant effect, the individual has the right to obtain MEANINGFUL HUMAN INTERVENTION, to make representations, and to contest the decision. Meaningful review = a trained/qualified human reviewing the actual outcome AFTER the automated decision, not tokenistic. Source: https://ico.org.uk/for-organisations/uk-gdpr-guidance-and-resources/individual-rights/automated-decision-making-and-profiling/
- LLM-facing correction works best as MACHINE-READABLE, directive feedback (precise, citing the specific rule and the offending span) so the model can regenerate — mirroring how guardrail frameworks return structured violations. Source: https://developer.nvidia.com/nemo-guardrails

REQUIRED CONTENT (EXACT headings):
## Two Explanation Styles — side-by-side. (a) CUSTOMER-FACING: polite, vague, non-technical, Consumer-Duty-safe, no rule leakage — with 2 concrete example messages. (b) LLM-FACING: precise, directive, cites the exact rule ID + offending span + how to fix, ideally structured (e.g. JSON) — with 2 concrete examples. Explain WHY they differ.
## AI Correction Loop — the return-for-correction cycle for the LLM: structured violation -> regenerate -> re-judge, with a RETRY BUDGET and what happens on exhaustion (escalate, safe-fallback, or block).
## Human-in-the-Loop — the Article 22 path: when a block/decision affects a customer significantly, how they get meaningful human review (trained reviewer, after the decision, able to contest), and how the customer-facing explanation routes them to it.
## Audit & Logging — what every judge decision records for accountability (decision, rule fired, direction, redactions) WITHOUT logging raw PII (data minimisation).

Strict on substance: the two styles must be genuinely different and each example must match its audience; the Article 22 human path must be present and meaningful.
tasks.check: f=report_feedback.md; grep -qiE '^##[[:space:]]*Two Explanation Styles' $f && grep -qiE 'customer.facing' $f && grep -qiE 'llm.facing|ai.facing' $f && grep -qiE '^##[[:space:]]*AI Correction Loop' $f && grep -qiE 'retry|regenerat' $f && grep -qiE '^##[[:space:]]*Human-in-the-Loop' $f && grep -qiE 'Article[[:space:]]*22|human review|human interven' $f && grep -qiE 'audit|logging' $f && echo 'feedback report well-formed' || { echo 'FAIL: report_feedback.md must have Two Explanation Styles (customer-facing vs LLM-facing w/ examples) + AI Correction Loop (retry) + Human-in-the-Loop (Article 22) + Audit/Logging'; cat $f 2>/dev/null; exit 1; }
tasks.verified: report_feedback.md: two distinct explanation styles with examples, AI self-correction retry loop, Article 22 human review path, PII-safe audit logging
